GK-CAN-001.name: Canna
GK-CAN-001.subtitle: Tropical foliage and flower impact for warm sheltered spots.

quickCare.Water: Water freely in growth; keep moist but not waterlogged
quickCare.Pruning: Cut stems to 15cm after first frost blackens foliage

GK-CAN-001.notes: Not hardy below roughly -2C. Lift and store rhizomes over winter in cold gardens, or grow in a pot and move under cover.
GK-CAN-001.soil: Rich, moisture-retentive soil with good structure.
GK-CAN-001.medium: Peat-free compost enriched with well-rotted manure or garden compost.

seasonalActions.Spring: Start rhizomes into growth under cover from March; plant out after last frost.
seasonalActions.Summer: Feed fortnightly and deadhead spent flowers.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back after blackened by frost; lift tender types.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store rhizomes frost-free and just barely moist, or protect in-ground with a thick mulch in mild areas.

problems.name: Slugs and snails
problems.fix: Shred young shoots; use barriers or torchlight removal early in the season.

problems.name: Canna virus (streaking/mottling)
problems.fix: No cure; discard and buy certified virus-free stock rather than treating.

problems.name: Poor flowering
problems.fix: Usually not enough sun or feed; move to a brighter spot and start a potash feed.

propagation.season: Divide dormant rhizomes in early spring before new growth starts.
propagation.methods: Divide rhizomes so each piece has at least one growing eye.
propagation.tips: Let cut surfaces callus for a day before potting to reduce rot risk.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Mainly ornamental; some varieties attract insects but value varies.

timeline.Pruning: Cut down after frost; overwinter rhizomes

gallery: Seed pod, fruit, tuber or spent flower if applicable

GK-CAN-001.weeklyFact: Canna is included in the GardenKey pilot set to test different plant habits and care needs.
GK-CAN-001.pollinatorFact: Canna flowers are bold and tropical-looking; value depends on variety and whether insects can access the flower parts.

GK-CAR-001.name: Carrot
GK-CAR-001.subtitle: Sweet roots for deep pots and fine soil.
GK-CAR-001.notes: Carrots are a good test for soil and medium notes because the root tells you exactly how good the growing conditions were.
GK-CAR-001.soil: Stone-free, light soil. Heavy or stony soil can fork the roots.
GK-CAR-001.medium: Fine compost mixed with sand or grit for containers. Avoid fresh manure.

seasonalActions.Summer: Water in dry spells and thin carefully.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store lifted roots or protect late crops.

problems.name: Forked roots
problems.fix: Usually caused by stones, compacted soil or rich fresh manure. Use a finer medium next time.

problems.name: Carrot fly
problems.fix: Cover with fine mesh and avoid bruising foliage when thinning.

propagation.season: Spring to early summer for most home sowings.
propagation.methods: Sow seed directly where it is to grow.
propagation.tips: Do not transplant carrots unless using special short-root types. They hate root disturbance.
propagation.tracker: Track sowing date, thinning date and first harvest.

pollinator.value: Carrot flowers are useful to insects if allowed to flower in the second year, but most crops are harvested before then.

GK-CAR-001.pollinatorFact: Umbellifer flowers like carrot relatives can be brilliant landing pads for hoverflies and other beneficial insects.
GK-CAR-001.weeklyFact: Carrot seed is tiny, which is why many gardeners accidentally sow enough carrots for a small village.

GK-COU-001.name: Courgette
GK-COU-001.subtitle: Big leaves, big flowers and regular summer picking.
GK-COU-001.notes: Courgettes are great for testing harvest tracking because fruit can go from perfect to comedy size very quickly.
GK-COU-001.medium: Peat-free compost with garden compost or manure-based improver in a large container.

seasonalActions.Summer: Water, feed and harvest small fruit regularly.
seasonalActions.Winter: Plan next year and compost healthy remains.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Common later in the season. Water the soil not the leaves, improve airflow and remove badly affected leaves.

problems.name: Poor fruit set
problems.fix: Often pollination or weather related. Encourage pollinators and try hand pollination if needed.

propagation.season: Sow in spring, plant out after frost risk.
propagation.methods: Seed in pots; plant out carefully without disturbing roots.
propagation.tips: Start with one or two plants unless you are emotionally prepared for courgette season.
propagation.tracker: Track sowing, first flower, first harvest and weekly harvest count.

pollinator.value: Large yellow flowers attract pollinators, and good pollination improves fruit set.

GK-COU-001.pollinatorFact: Courgettes have male and female flowers, and bees do a lot of the matchmaking.
GK-COU-001.weeklyFact: A courgette can hide under one leaf and become a marrow while you are making a cup of tea.

GK-DAH-001.name: Dahlia
GK-DAH-001.subtitle: Bold late-summer colour from tubers.

quickCare.Water: Water well in dry spells once established
quickCare.Light: Full sun, at least 6 hours a day
quickCare.Feeding: Weekly high-potash feed once buds form
quickCare.Pruning: Deadhead constantly; pinch young plants to bush out

GK-DAH-001.notes: Stake early — dahlias put on height fast and snap in wind once top-heavy with flower.
GK-DAH-001.soil: Fertile, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter.
GK-DAH-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for containers; needs regular feeding as it's hungry.

seasonalActions.Spring: Pot up tubers under cover from March; plant out after last frost.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead weekly, feed and stake as growth continues.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Lift tubers after the first frost blackens foliage, or mulch heavily to overwinter in mild, free-draining gardens.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store lifted tubers somewhere frost-free, dark and just barely moist.

problems.name: Earwigs
problems.fix: Chew petal edges at night. Trap in upturned straw-filled pots on canes, empty in the morning.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Worse late season; improve airflow and water at the base, not the leaves.

problems.name: Slugs on young shoots
problems.fix: Protect new spring growth with barriers or torchlight removal.

propagation.season: Divide dormant tubers in spring, just as eyes start to show.
propagation.methods: Divide tuber clumps so each division has a piece of the old stem base with at least one eye.
propagation.tips: Basal cuttings taken from sprouted tubers in early spring multiply stock fastest.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Open-centred dahlias are better for pollinators than very double forms.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead summer; lift tubers after frost

gallery: Seed pod, fruit, tuber or spent flower if applicable

GK-DAH-001.weeklyFact: Dahlia is included in the GardenKey pilot set to test different plant habits and care needs.
GK-DAH-001.pollinatorFact: Open-centred dahlias are much better for bees than tight decorative doubles, which can hide the pollen and nectar.

GK-FRN-001.name: Fern
GK-FRN-001.subtitle: Shade-loving foliage for calm, green corners.
GK-FRN-001.notes: Ferns prefer consistent moisture and shelter from drying winds. Avoid strong afternoon sun.
GK-FRN-001.soil: Humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil with good structure.
GK-FRN-001.medium: Peat-free multipurpose compost mixed with leaf mould or fine bark for moisture retention.

seasonalActions.Spring: Remove old fronds as new growth appears.
seasonalActions.Winter: Tidy dead fronds when the plant is dormant or growth has slowed, usually November to March. Leaving some cover can help protect the crown.

problems.name: Crispy fronds
problems.fix: Increase humidity/moisture and move from direct sun.

problems.name: Slug damage
problems.fix: Check under rims and use barriers/traps rather than harsh chemicals.

propagation.methods: Divide established clumps or grow from spores for advanced propagation.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Mainly habitat and shelter value rather than nectar value.

photoPlan.hero: Fresh fronds with shaded pot/garden context.

GK-FRN-001.weeklyFact: Ferns are ancient plants that reproduce by spores rather than flowers — very calm, very green, very Jurassic.
GK-FRN-001.pollinatorFact: Ferns do not provide flowers for pollinators, but they do create shelter and humidity for small garden life.

GK-HOS-001.name: Hosta
GK-HOS-001.subtitle: Bold shade foliage that slugs love just as much as you do.

quickCare.Water: Keep consistently moist, never let dry out fully
quickCare.Light: Part to full shade; morning sun is fine, avoid midday sun
quickCare.Feeding: Balanced liquid feed monthly through spring and summer
quickCare.Pruning: Cut all foliage to the ground once it dies back in autumn

GK-HOS-001.notes: The biggest threat to hostas is slugs and snails, not disease — protect emerging shoots in spring above anything else.
GK-HOS-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added leaf mould or well-rotted organic matter for moisture retention.

seasonalActions.Spring: Protect emerging shoots from slugs immediately — this is the most vulnerable stage.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water in dry spells and keep feeding monthly.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Cut back foliage once it yellows and collapses after first frosts.
seasonalActions.Winter: Dormant and fully hardy; no protection needed outdoors.

problems.name: Slugs and snails
problems.fix: The main hosta problem. Use copper tape, grit barriers, beer traps or torchlight removal at night, especially on new spring shoots.

problems.name: Sun scorch
problems.fix: Pale, crispy leaf edges mean too much direct sun — move to deeper shade.

problems.name: Virus (mottled/streaked leaves)
problems.fix: No cure; remove and destroy affected plants rather than propagating from them.

propagation.season: Divide dormant clumps in early spring or autumn.
propagation.methods: Lift the whole clump and divide with a spade or knife, keeping several growth points per division.
propagation.tips: Water divisions in well and keep moist until re-established.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Tubular summer flowers are visited by bees and occasionally hummingbird-mimicking hawkmoths at dusk.

gallery: Slug damage if present, for reference

GK-HOS-001.weeklyFact: Some hosta leaves can grow large enough to hold a full cup of rainwater after a summer shower.
GK-HOS-001.pollinatorFact: Hosta's tubular flowers are shaped for bees and long-tongued pollinators, and are sometimes visited by moths at dusk.

GK-HYD-001.name: Hydrangea
GK-HYD-001.subtitle: Big summer flowerheads that shift colour with soil pH.

quickCare.Water: Water generously, especially in pots — wilts fast when dry
quickCare.Feeding: Spring feed with an ericaceous formula if you want blue flowers
quickCare.Pruning: Cut spent flowerheads to the first strong bud pair in spring, not autumn

GK-HYD-001.notes: For blue flowers the soil needs to be acidic with available aluminium; for pink, more alkaline. White varieties don't change colour regardless of soil.
GK-HYD-001.soil: Moist, fertile, humus-rich soil. Acidic soil produces blue flowers on macrophylla types; alkaline produces pink.
GK-HYD-001.medium: Ericaceous peat-free compost for pots if aiming for blue flowers; standard peat-free compost for pink or white.

seasonalActions.Spring: Prune out old flowerheads to the first healthy bud pair once new growth is visible.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water generously, especially in containers, and deadhead as flowers fade.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leave old flowerheads on over winter — they protect next year's buds from frost.
seasonalActions.Winter: Leave faded flowerheads in place for frost protection; do not prune now.

problems.name: Wilting in pots
problems.fix: Hydrangeas in containers dry out fast in summer heat; water daily in hot spells or move to part shade.

problems.name: No flowers
problems.fix: Usually pruned at the wrong time (cutting off next year's buds) or frost damage to buds — check pruning timing.

problems.name: Leaf scorch
problems.fix: Too much direct afternoon sun; move to a spot with afternoon shade.

propagation.methods: Take non-flowering shoot tips, remove lower leaves, root in a free-draining cutting mix.
propagation.tips: Keep cuttings humid (a clear bag works) until rooted, then pot on individually.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Mophead varieties are mostly sterile ornamental flowers; lacecap types offer more accessible pollen for insects.

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Hydrangea in flower.
gallery: Colour comparison if soil pH varies across the garden

GK-HYD-001.weeklyFact: A hydrangea's flower colour is a live pH test for your soil — blue means acidic, pink means alkaline.
GK-HYD-001.pollinatorFact: Lacecap hydrangeas have small fertile flowers around the rim that are far more useful to pollinators than the showy sterile mophead types.

GK-ACE-001.name: Japanese Maple
GK-ACE-001.subtitle: Elegant, slow-growing tree prized for foliage and autumn colour.

quickCare.Water: Keep evenly moist, especially in pots — never let it dry out or waterlog
quickCare.Light: Dappled or part shade, sheltered from strong wind and hot midday sun
quickCare.Feeding: Light, slow-release feed in spring only — avoid high nitrogen
quickCare.Pruning: Minimal — remove dead or crossing branches in late autumn/winter while dormant

GK-ACE-001.notes: Leaf edges scorch easily in strong wind, hot sun or drought — shelter is as important as soil.
GK-ACE-001.soil: Humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil.
GK-ACE-001.medium: Peat-free ericaceous or acid-friendly compost for pots, with excellent drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Watch for late frost damage on new leaves; feed lightly once.
seasonalActions.Summer: Water consistently and shelter from scorching sun and wind.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Enjoy the colour change; keep roots moist as leaves turn.
seasonalActions.Winter: Prune dead or crossing wood only while fully dormant; protect pots from freezing solid.

problems.name: Leaf scorch
problems.fix: Brown, crispy leaf edges from wind, sun or drought — move to a more sheltered spot and water consistently.

problems.name: Verticillium wilt
problems.fix: Sudden branch dieback; remove affected wood promptly and avoid replanting maples in the same spot.

problems.name: Aphids on new growth
problems.fix: Wash off with water or encourage ladybirds; rarely serious.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in early summer, or grafting for named cultivars.
propagation.methods: Named varieties are usually grafted; species types can be grown from seed sown fresh in autumn.
propagation.tips: Seed-grown maples won't come true to a named cultivar — expect variation.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Small spring flowers offer modest early-season nectar and pollen for insects before the canopy is fully out.

timeline.Seeding: Sow fresh seed in autumn if species type

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Japanese Maple, ideally showing canopy shape.

GK-ACE-001.weeklyFact: Japanese maples can live for well over a century and are often trained as bonsai for exactly that reason.
GK-ACE-001.pollinatorFact: The small red-green spring flowers are easy to miss but provide early nectar before many other plants are in bloom.

GK-LAV-001.name: Lavender
GK-LAV-001.subtitle: Scented, sun-loving, pollinator friendly.
GK-LAV-001.notes: Do not overwater. Lavender dislikes sitting in wet compost. Add grit or perlite if the potting mix is too rich or heavy.
GK-LAV-001.soil: Free-draining, gritty, slightly alkaline to neutral soil. Avoid heavy wet compost.
GK-LAV-001.medium: Peat-free compost mixed with horticultural grit or perlite. For pots, aim for sharp drainage and raise the pot slightly in winter.

seasonalActions.Spring: Check drainage, remove dead growth and lightly refresh top dressing.
seasonalActions.Summer: Trim lightly after flowering and avoid heavy feeding.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Reduce watering and keep the crown dry.
seasonalActions.Winter: Keep it on the dry side and avoid waterlogged compost. If pruning is needed, wait until the plant is dormant or growth is quiet — usually November to March — but avoid cutting hard into old woody stems.

problems.name: Root rot
problems.fix: Usually caused by wet compost. Improve drainage, reduce watering and consider repotting into a gritty mix.

problems.name: Poor flowering
problems.fix: Move to stronger sun and avoid rich feeding.

problems.name: Aphids
problems.fix: Squash by hand or wash off with water; encourage hoverflies and ladybirds.

propagation.season: Late summer for semi-ripe cuttings; spring for seed.
propagation.methods: Semi-ripe cuttings root well in a gritty, free-draining medium.
propagation.tips: Avoid overly humid sealed conditions; gentle ventilation helps reduce rot.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Excellent nectar source for bees, butterflies and hoverflies. Flowering lavender can support pollinators while ornamentals nearby provide colour and structure.

photoPlan.hero: Whole plant in flower, ideally with bees visible.

GK-LAV-001.weeklyFact: Lavender has been used for centuries in gardens, medicine cupboards and linen drawers — basically the original plant-based air freshener.
GK-LAV-001.pollinatorFact: Open lavender flowers are brilliant for bees because the nectar is easy to access and the plant flowers over a useful summer window.

GK-LET-001.name: Lettuce
GK-LET-001.subtitle: Fast, useful salad leaves for pots and beds.
GK-LET-001.notes: Lettuce is a good tester plant because it changes quickly. It shows whether GardenKey is useful for repeat jobs like watering, harvesting and sowing follow-up batches.
GK-LET-001.soil: Moist, fertile, free-draining soil. Avoid letting containers dry out completely.
GK-LET-001.medium: Peat-free multipurpose compost with a little fine compost or worm cast for moisture holding.

seasonalActions.Summer: Give light shade in hot spells and harvest regularly.
seasonalActions.Winter: Growth slows; protect from harsh weather and wet compost.

problems.name: Bolting
problems.fix: Heat or stress can make lettuce run to seed. Sow little and often, keep evenly moist and use shade in hot weather.

problems.name: Slugs
problems.fix: Use barriers, night checks and tidy hiding places. Avoid pellets where pets/wildlife may be affected.

propagation.season: Spring to autumn, depending on variety.
propagation.methods: Sow seed thinly in trays, modules or directly in containers.
propagation.tips: Label each sowing date. Small batches are better than one huge glut.
propagation.tracker: Easy future tracker: sowing date, first pick date, flavour and whether it bolted.

pollinator.value: Lettuce is mainly grown for leaves, not pollinators, but leaving one plant to flower can feed small insects.

GK-LET-001.pollinatorFact: If one lettuce bolts, leave it briefly and watch how many tiny insects visit the flowers.
GK-LET-001.weeklyFact: Lettuce is one of the best plants for testing a garden app because you can see results in weeks, not years.

GK-POT-001.name: Potato
GK-POT-001.subtitle: A satisfying crop for bags, tubs and beds.
GK-POT-001.notes: Potatoes are good for beginners because the harvest feels like treasure hunting.
GK-POT-001.medium: Peat-free compost mixed with garden compost in a deep bag or large tub.

seasonalActions.Summer: Water well, earth up and harvest earlies.
seasonalActions.Winter: Store in a cool dark place.

problems.name: Blight
problems.fix: Remove affected foliage and avoid wet leaves. Choose resistant varieties where blight is common.

problems.name: Scab
problems.fix: Often worse in dry or alkaline conditions. Keep moisture steady while tubers form.

propagation.methods: Seed potatoes planted in bags, tubs or beds.
propagation.tips: Do not use supermarket potatoes for trials if you want clean, predictable results.
propagation.tracker: Track planting date, earthing-up dates and harvest weight.

pollinator.value: Potato flowers can attract insects, but most value is food production rather than ornamental pollinator support.

GK-POT-001.pollinatorFact: Potato flowers are not the main reason to grow them for wildlife, but they can still bring insect visitors.
GK-POT-001.weeklyFact: Potatoes are related to tomatoes, which explains why potato flowers can look strangely familiar.

GK-ROS-001.name: Rose
GK-ROS-001.subtitle: Classic flowering shrub with high ornamental value.

quickCare.Feeding: Feed in spring and early summer

GK-ROS-001.notes: Deadhead regularly to encourage flowering. Keep air moving around the plant to reduce fungal disease.
GK-ROS-001.soil: Fertile, moisture-retentive but free-draining soil. Avoid waterlogging.
GK-ROS-001.medium: For pots, use a loam-based peat-free compost with added organic matter and good drainage.

seasonalActions.Spring: Feed and mulch; check new shoots for aphids.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead, water deeply in dry periods and watch for blackspot.
seasonalActions.Winter: Prune when dormant, usually November to March. Aim for an open shape, remove dead or crossing stems, and use clean secateurs.

problems.name: Blackspot
problems.fix: Remove affected leaves, avoid overhead watering and improve airflow.

problems.name: Aphids
problems.fix: Wipe off early colonies or spray with water; avoid broad insecticides where possible.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Water consistently, improve airflow and avoid drought stress.

propagation.season: Hardwood cuttings in autumn or winter.
propagation.methods: Take pencil-thick cuttings from healthy stems and insert into gritty compost.
propagation.tips: Label varieties carefully; many roses look similar when dormant.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: Single and semi-double roses are usually better for pollinators than very double flowers.

photoPlan.hero: Open flower and whole shrub context.

GK-ROS-001.weeklyFact: Open-centred roses can provide pollen access for bees, unlike many very double varieties.
GK-ROS-001.pollinatorFact: Single and semi-double roses are usually more useful to pollinators than very full double roses, because insects can actually reach the centre.

GK-SAL-001.name: Salvia
GK-SAL-001.subtitle: Spiky, long-flowering perennial that bees can't get enough of.

quickCare.Water: Drought-tolerant once established; water in prolonged dry spells only
quickCare.Feeding: Light spring feed — too rich a diet causes floppy growth
quickCare.Pruning: Cut spent flower spikes to encourage a second flush; cut back hard in spring

GK-SAL-001.notes: Deadheading spent spikes is the single biggest thing you can do to keep salvia flowering all summer.
GK-SAL-001.soil: Free-draining soil; tolerates poor soil better than rich, wet ground.
GK-SAL-001.medium: Peat-free compost with added grit for sharp drainage, especially in containers.

seasonalActions.Spring: Cut old growth right back to fresh shoots at the base.
seasonalActions.Summer: Deadhead spent spikes regularly to prolong flowering into autumn.
seasonalActions.Autumn: Leave the last flush of growth in place over winter for frost protection.
seasonalActions.Winter: Leave old stems standing — cutting back now exposes the crown to frost damage.

problems.name: Floppy growth
problems.fix: Usually too much shade or too rich a feed; move to full sun and ease off feeding.

problems.name: Powdery mildew
problems.fix: Improve airflow and avoid overhead watering in humid weather.

problems.name: Winter losses in wet soil
problems.fix: Poor drainage is the main killer, not cold. Improve drainage or grow in pots in heavy clay gardens.

propagation.season: Softwood cuttings in late spring, or division in spring.
propagation.methods: Take non-flowering shoot tips for cuttings, or divide established clumps.
propagation.tips: Cuttings root quickly in a free-draining mix on a warm windowsill.
propagation.tracker: Propagation tracker coming soon — for now, add dates, method and result in your notes.

pollinator.value: One of the best border plants for bees — long tubular flowers over a very long season.

timeline.Pruning: Deadhead through summer; cut back hard in spring

photoPlan.hero: Clear whole-plant photo of Salvia in flower.
gallery: Bees or pollinators visiting if captured

GK-SAL-001.weeklyFact: Salvia's tubular flowers are shaped almost exactly for a bumblebee's tongue length.
GK-SAL-001.pollinatorFact: Salvia is consistently one of the top-rated plants in pollinator surveys thanks to its long flowering season and easy nectar access.
